prs.title: Fix `PropertyBinding` crash when `morphTargetDictionary` is undefined
prs.body:
**Description:**

Hello again! Still poking around the edges of the codebase. I ran into a lifecycle edge case in `PropertyBinding.bind()` that throws a fatal error, and wanted to get a safety guard in place for it.

**The Problem:**
If a user adds `morphAttributes` to a geometry *after* a Mesh is constructed, but hasn't yet called `updateMorphTargets()`, the `mesh.morphTargetDictionary` remains `undefined` (its default state). 

If an animation mixer tries to bind to a named morph target during this gap (e.g., trying to resolve `.morphTargetInfluences[smile]`), `PropertyBinding` attempts to read the name key directly from the undefined dictionary, resulting in a hard crash: `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`.

**The Fix:**
I added a simple safety check to fail gracefully if the dictionary is missing. Instead of throwing a fatal error, it aborts the binding cleanly. 

**Testing:**
I also added a QUnit test to `test/unit/src/animation/PropertyBinding.tests.js` that recreates this exact mesh lifecycle gap to ensure it safely bypasses the throw.

fix(utils): wrap discriminator merge check in parentheses to fix precedence
prs.body:
**Summary**:-

While going through `lib/utils.js`, I noticed a regression in `merge()` that seems to have been introduced with the changes from gh-9534. The issue comes down to JavaScript operator precedence — the `isDiscriminatorSchemaMerge` check was only affecting part of the condition, not the whole thing.

**The issue**:-

Right now the condition is effectively evaluated as:

`(options.isDiscriminatorSchemaMerge && A) || B`

Because of that, whenever Condition B is true (the array vs. single nested mismatch case), the merge gets skipped even if `isDiscriminatorSchemaMerge` is false. That doesn’t seem intentional, and in practice it can cause merges to silently drop data in normal (non-discriminator) scenarios that happen to hit this edge case.

**The fix**:-

I added parentheses to group the branches, ensuring the guard covers the entire mismatch logic.

`options.isDiscriminatorSchemaMerge && (ConditionA || ConditionB)`

This keeps the behavior introduced in gh-9534, but limits it to actual discriminator merges, which seems to be the original intent.
While I was here, I also checked `hasKey` and `mergeClone` for similar patterns, but didn’t see the same issue there.

**Tests**:-

Added a couple of regression tests in `test/utils.test.js` to make sure this only triggers when `isDiscriminatorSchemaMerge` is true, and to prevent this from slipping back in later.

prs.title: cli: check file close errors in tsdump
prs.body:
`debugTimeSeriesDumpCmd` was using a naked `defer f.Close()`, which swallowed any errors returned when flushing the output file to disk. Because file writes are buffered, an issue like a full disk might only manifest when the file is actually closed.

This commit wraps the defer in a closure to explicitly capture the `Close()` error. This ensures the CLI actually fails and returns the error instead of exiting with a success code on a truncated dump.

Epic: None

Release note (cli change): Fixed a bug in the `cockroach debug ts-dump` command where file close errors were silently ignored, potentially masking data truncation.

prs.title: security: switch join token generation to crypto/rand
prs.body:
The previous implementation used math/rand with a time-based seed to generate the shared secret for cluster join tokens. This approach is predictable and not suitable for security-sensitive use cases, making the tokens vulnerable to brute-force attacks.

This change replaces math/rand with crypto/rand to ensure the shared secret is generated using a cryptographically secure random source. While this introduces a minor dependency on OS entropy, the impact is negligible since join tokens are created infrequently.

Release note (security update): Improve the security of cluster join tokens by generating shared secrets using a cryptographically secure random number generator.

labels.name: docs
labels.description: This issue is due to a mistake or omission in the mongoosejs.com documentation

labels.name: typescript
labels.description: Types or Types-test related issue / Pull Request

prs.body:
**PR Title:**
`Docs: Add guidance on using loadClass with TypeScript (fixes #12813)`

**PR Description:**

**Summary**

This PR addresses [[issue #12813](https://github.com/Automattic/mongoose/issues/12813)](https://github.com/Automattic/mongoose/issues/12813), which pointed out that it's confusing for developers to figure out how to use `schema.loadClass()` correctly with TypeScript.

To fix this, I've added a new documentation section to **`docs/typescript/statics-and-methods.md`** that provides clear, official guidance.

This new section covers the complete pattern discussed in the issue thread, including:
* **The Basic Pattern:** How to manually combine types for the Schema, Class, Model (`Model<Combined> & typeof Class`), and Document (`Document & Combined`).
* **Typing `this`:** The correct way to type the `this` parameter in both instance and static methods.
* **Known Limitations:** Explicitly documents the main caveats:
    * That `this` parameters aren't supported in getters/setters (ts(2784)).
    * The "lean" caveat: how `toObject()`, `toJSON()`, or `.lean()` return plain objects without the class methods, which can lead to runtime errors.

To validate this new documentation and prevent future regressions, this PR also adds a new `tsd` test file at **`test/types/docs-loadClass.test.ts`**.

**Examples**

The new documentation page itself contains all the code examples:
* See the new `## Using schema.loadClass()` section in **`docs/typescript/statics-and-methods.md`**.

The new `tsd` test file also serves as a practical, working example of these patterns:
* See **`test/types/docs-loadClass.test.ts`**.

---
✅ Adds documentation
✅ Adds type tests
✅ Fixes #12813

prs.title: Celsius_Fahrenheit_Calculator.py
prs.body:
This is a simple celsius to fahrenheit calculator.

Instruction for use:
The input contains an integer "is_fahrenheit" and floating point input "temperature", where temperature represents temperature in degrees celsius when is_fahrenheit=0 and temperature in degrees Fahrenheit when is_fahrenheit=1.

The result is rounded of to 4 decimal places for a precise calculation.
Thank You , I hope this is useful.

issues.title: Pollinations API returns HTTP 402 (Insufficient balance) despite positive account balance
issues.body:
### Issue
Requests to Pollinations API using `gemini-fast` fail with HTTP 402 (Insufficient balance), even though the account shows a positive balance.

### Error
[AI Queue] Failed with gemini-fast (Attempt 1)
Reason: Pollinations HTTP 402: Insufficient balance.
This model costs ~0.0002 pollen per request, but your available balance is 0.0000.

### Expected behavior
API should allow requests when account balance is positive.

### Actual behavior
API reports available balance as 0.0000 and rejects requests.

### Notes
- Account dashboard shows positive balance (about 0.375 pollens)
- Issue persists across retries
- Possible mismatch between displayed balance and API balance

issues.title: [App Submission] Website Studio
issues.body:
### App Name

Website Studio 

### App Description

**Website Studio** is a comprehensive resource and review platform designed to help local business owners—primarily across the USA—find the absolute best website building platforms for their specific industry and city. From fitness centers in Florida to plumbers in Texas, we provide hyper-local, deeply tailored guides and competitor comparisons to help small businesses successfully launch their digital presence.

To achieve this level of local specificity at a massive scale, the platform is powered by a custom-built Programmatic SEO (pSEO) engine managed exclusively by our internal admin team.

**How we use Pollinations.ai:**
We utilize the `pollinations.ai` API strictly inside our asynchronous background worker queue (powered by Node.js, BullMQ, and Redis). Our admin system queues up thousands of geographic and industry-specific keywords, and the Pollinations API acts as our core heavy-lifting text engine. It autonomously writes highly optimized, schema-compliant, long-form articles complete with local case studies, JSON-LD structured data, and targeted meta tags.

Because of the speed and reliability of the Pollinations API, our backend automated pipeline is actively on track to generate and publish **50,000 highly targeted local SEO blogs over the next 3 months**, scaling our platform to cover almost every major local business sector in the United States.

### App URL

https://website-studio.onrender.com/

### GitHub Open Source Repository URL

https://github.com/Necro-Rohan/Website-Studio

### Discord Username

_No response_

### App Language

_No response_

### Email / Other Contact

rohangupta5671@gmail.com

issues.title: Tier: Clarification on Spore -> Seed upgrade timing and dev point tracking
issues.body:
### Current Tier

Spore (0.01 pollen/hour)

### Question

Hi team! 

I'm currently building an app using the Pollinations image API and I'm about 85% done. However, I'm starting to hit the API rate limits on my current Spore tier, which is slowing down my testing, so I'm looking to upgrade to the Seed tier to keep the momentum going.

I have a couple of quick questions about how the upgrade process works:

1. **Upgrade Timing:** The dashboard mentions that the Seed tier is "Auto-upgraded weekly." Does this mean upgrades happen on a fixed global day of the week (e.g., every Monday), or is it calculated exactly 7 days after my specific account registration/verification?
2. **Dev Points Tracking:** To unlock the Seed tier, I see I need "8+ dev points." Is there currently any way in the dashboard (or via an endpoint) to check my progress and see exactly how many dev points I have accumulated so far?

Thanks for the help and for building such an awesome tool!
